name: BroScienceBench
description: Myth-vs-evidence MC benchmark for LLM strength-training advice. 246 items across 7 categories (mechanism, practice, bodycomp, nutrition, safety, recovery, calibration), incl. adversarial phrasings, calibration (overclaiming) items, hard items, and held-out myths (not named in ALI's guardrails) to test generalization vs pattern-matching. First 150 items are identical to v1.0.
grading: exact-match on option letter (MCQ) or LLM-judge (open). accuracy=chose answer; myth_adherence=chose bro_science_option. For calibration items, bro_science_option = the overclaim.

items.question: Can I confidently state one MEV for everybody?

options.A: There's a settled, universal minimum-effective-volume number everyone should follow.
options.B: Volume has no minimum and any amount works equally for everyone.
options.C: 'Minimum effective volume' is a useful concept, but a single settled universal number doesn't exist — it varies by person, muscle, and context; treating it as a fixed law overclaims.

items.question: Is the minimum effective volume a solved, universal figure?

options.A: There's a settled, universal minimum-effective-volume number everyone should follow.
options.B: Volume has no minimum and any amount works equally for everyone.
options.C: 'Minimum effective volume' is a useful concept, but a single settled universal number doesn't exist — it varies by person, muscle, and context; treating it as a fixed law overclaims.

myth_evidence.anabolicwindow: The post-exercise 'anabolic window' is far wider than claimed; total daily protein matters much more than precise timing for most lifters.
myth_evidence.bcaa: With adequate total protein, BCAA supplements are largely unnecessary; whole-protein sources already provide them. One of the more oversold supplements.
myth_evidence.caffeinecycle: Caffeine tolerance develops with daily use, attenuating acute effects; strategic reduction can restore sensitivity but is not obligatory, and the benefit is partially preserved with tolerance.
myth_evidence.carbsnight: Carbohydrates eaten at night are not preferentially stored as fat; total daily caloric balance, not time of day, drives fat gain or loss.
myth_evidence.cardiokillsmuscle: Moderate concurrent training does not meaningfully impair hypertrophy when protein and calories are adequate; only very high endurance volumes interfere.
myth_evidence.cleaneating: Body composition is primarily determined by total energy balance and macronutrients, not food 'cleanliness'; whole foods aid micronutrients/satiety but 'clean/dirty' has no direct body-comp mechanism.
myth_evidence.creatineload: A loading phase is not required; ~3-5 g/day saturates creatine stores within weeks. Loading only speeds saturation and is optional.
myth_evidence.creatinesteroid: Creatine monohydrate is not a steroid and does not harm healthy kidneys; it is among the most evidence-supported, safe supplements (~3-5 g/day).
myth_evidence.deload: Deloading manages accumulated fatigue, but evidence for a fixed every-4-6-week schedule is weak; need varies by individual, intensity, volume, and recovery. A reasonable practice, not a settled prescription.
myth_evidence.eggyolks: Dietary cholesterol from egg yolks has minimal impact on blood LDL in most people; yolks hold most of an egg's micronutrients and discarding them is unnecessary for most healthy individuals.
myth_evidence.failure: Stopping ~0-3 reps from failure captures most of the hypertrophic stimulus; training to failure every set adds disproportionate fatigue for little extra gain.
myth_evidence.fastedcardio: Fasted cardio may oxidise more fat acutely, but 24-hour fat balance is set by total energy deficit; fasted vs fed does not meaningfully change fat loss.
myth_evidence.fatburningzone: The low-intensity 'fat-burning zone' oxidises a higher fraction of fat per minute but fewer total calories; 24-hour fat balance is governed by total deficit, not exercise substrate use.
myth_evidence.fullrom: Full range of motion (especially at long muscle lengths) generally builds at least as much muscle as partials; consistently using heavier partial reps is not optimal for growth.
myth_evidence.geneticceiling: Genetics set an upper ceiling, but most people are far from it even after years of training; the '2-3 year natural ceiling' is not evidence-supported. Progress slows but continues.
myth_evidence.glutamine: Glutamine supplementation does not improve muscle recovery or growth in healthy individuals consuming adequate protein; clinical uses exist but it is largely unnecessary for athletes.
myth_evidence.hiit_always: HIIT and steady-state cardio produce similar fat-loss outcomes when matched for energy expenditure; HIIT is more time-efficient but more fatiguing. Best choice depends on context.
myth_evidence.icebath: Cold water immersion blunts inflammatory signalling that partly drives hypertrophic adaptation; it may aid acute soreness but attenuates long-term strength/muscle gains after resistance training.
myth_evidence.lengthened: Training at long muscle lengths is promising and increasingly supported, but not a fully settled verdict; a reasonable bias, not a proven universal rule.
myth_evidence.machines: Machines and free weights are different tools with different loading profiles; both build muscle effectively, neither is inherently superior.
myth_evidence.mealfreq: Meal frequency has little effect on metabolism or fat loss; total daily intake dominates. Eating 6 small meals does not 'stoke' metabolism.
myth_evidence.mev: Minimum effective volume is a useful heuristic, but the precise per-muscle values are not empirically settled and vary by individual, muscle, and training history. A framework, not a proven prescription.
myth_evidence.muscleconfusion: Muscle does not get 'confused'; consistent progressive overload on stable exercises drives growth. Constant random variation can impair progress tracking.
myth_evidence.musclefat: Muscle and fat are different tissues; one cannot convert to the other. Detraining can cause muscle loss and fat gain separately, not a conversion.
myth_evidence.musclememory: Muscle memory is real and mechanistically supported: myonuclei gained during training are retained after detraining, allowing faster regrowth upon retraining.
myth_evidence.nopainogain: Sharp, joint, or persistent pain signals potential injury, not productive training stress. Muscular fatigue and mild burn are normal; true pain is a stop signal.
myth_evidence.overload: Adding load is the method to maintain an adequate stimulus as you adapt; progressive overload tracks/sustains growth rather than directly causing it.
myth_evidence.proteinperpound: ~1.6-2.2 g/kg/day maximizes muscle protein synthesis; ~1 g per pound is an upper bound, not a strict requirement.
myth_evidence.pump: The pump is transient cell swelling from blood/fluid; it is a byproduct, not a validated proxy for muscle growth.
myth_evidence.recomp: Body recomposition (simultaneous muscle gain and fat loss) is achievable, especially for beginners, returnees, or higher-body-fat individuals with adequate protein/training.
myth_evidence.shortrest: ~2-3 min rest on compounds preserves load/reps and is equal or better for hypertrophy than short rests.
myth_evidence.sleepsettled: Sleep is strongly associated with anabolic hormones and recovery, and deprivation impairs performance/body-composition; however, the precise causal dose-response on hypertrophy is not fully settled.
myth_evidence.slowreps: A controlled eccentric is fine, but deliberately inflating tempo/TUT is not a growth driver and reduces loadable volume.
myth_evidence.soreness: DOMS reflects novel/eccentric stress, not growth; it is not a reliable proxy for hypertrophy. Performance progression is more informative.
myth_evidence.spotreduce: Spot reduction is unsupported; fat loss is systemic and governed by total energy balance, not the muscle trained.
myth_evidence.squatdepth: For healthy lifters, squatting below parallel is safe and effective; deep squats do not inherently damage knees.
myth_evidence.staticstretch: Prolonged static stretching immediately pre-lift acutely reduces force/power; a dynamic warm-up is preferable. Post-workout static stretching for flexibility is fine.
myth_evidence.teengrowth: Properly coached resistance training is safe for adolescents and does not stunt growth; the 'stunts growth' claim is unsupported.
myth_evidence.toning: 'Toning' is not a distinct outcome; you build muscle and lose fat. Rep range does not change muscle 'length/shape' (largely genetic).
myth_evidence.tut: Mechanical tension from challenging reps near failure is the primary hypertrophy driver; time-under-tension and metabolite (lactic-acid) accumulation are not the main cause.
myth_evidence.volume: Higher volume generally drives more growth up to a point, but the upper end of the dose-response is contested with diminishing returns; not a 'more is always better' law.
myth_evidence.womenbulky: Most women's hormonal profile makes rapid large hypertrophy unlikely; resistance training builds strength and a lean, muscular physique, not quick bulk.

evidence_note: One-line evidence summary per myth cluster; supporting citations are in scripts/citations.md.
